summary: Benn argues that the traditional concept of a 'pivot' — a heavy, deliberate change in company direction — may be obsolete in an era where software is cheap to build and markets shift constantly. He suggests companies may need to operate more like ice cream shops or musicians, constantly experimenting rather than seeking a durable direction, while also exploring how Allbirds' absurd pivot to AI reveals a new model where companies become portable brands rather than means of production.
key_points: The traditional pivot carries implicit assumptions — that companies need clear direction, that changing course is heavy and usually fails — that may no longer apply when software is cheap and fast to build
When building is cheap, commitment becomes expensive and constant experimentation becomes the strategy, inverting the old rules
Allbirds' pivot to AI, while seemingly absurd, illustrates how companies may be evolving from means of production into portable brands, much like how celebrities became influencers
The generation that built careers on careful strategic commitment may be poorly equipped for a world where everything is immediately possible and nothing is permanent
The language we use ('pivot') loads situations with outdated implications that prevent us from seeing the new reality clearly
money_quotes: Commitment becomes expensive, and pivoting is what's important.

Maybe we do not need a direction; we need to just keep moving. Maybe we cannot hide from Anthropic and OpenAI; we can only keep running from them. Maybe we aren't pivoting; we're just playing Pac-Man.
You've got a great name, you've got a great team, you've got a great logo, and you've got a great name. Now you just need an idea — over and over and over again.
If people can become brands, maybe brands can become brands.
When you're born in the darkness, the light might be nothing but blinding.
key_insight: The era of deliberate, weighty pivots is giving way to a world where companies must operate as perpetual test kitchens — constantly experimenting not as a sign of failure, but as the entire strategy — and the brands that survive may be those that become portable identities detached from any single product.
